summary: Apple announced DTrace support for Mac OS X Leopard at WWDC, representing a significant engineering achievement that includes implementing the FBT provider for PowerPC. Cantrill celebrates this as validation of the OpenSolaris community's open source approach, directly countering IBM's criticism that they weren't operating in the 'spirit' of open source. The port, while not yet complete, is functional enough to be useful and will bring DTrace to a much broader audience of developers.
key_points: Apple ported DTrace to Mac OS X Leopard with substantial engineering work
The port includes FBT provider implementation for PowerPC architecture
Apple is using DTrace as foundation for their new Xray performance tool
The collaboration validates OpenSolaris community's open source approach
money_quotes: Often (or even usually?) announcements at conferences are more vapor than ware. In this case, though, Apple is being quite modest: they have done a tremendous amount of engineering work to bring DTrace to their platform.

The entire OpenSolaris community should feel proud about this development because it gives the lie to IBM's nauseating assertion that we in the OpenSolaris community aren't in the 'spirit' of open source.
It will be a relief to finally have DTrace on the only computer under my roof that doesn't run Solaris!
While it's not yet a complete port, it's certainly enough to be uniquely useful – and it was quite a thrill to see Objective C frames in a ustack action!
key_insight: Cross-platform adoption of open source technology like DTrace validates the collaborative spirit of open source communities, regardless of corporate criticism about licensing approaches.
